chunk_summary: Guidance on enteral feeding escalation for children with neurodisability emphasizes safety, sufficiency, and family feasibility, with thresholds for oral feeding and staged transitions to tube feeding based on clinical and caregiver needs.
learning_objectives: Understand thresholds for transitioning from oral to enteral feeding in children with neurodisability.
Learn the benefits and limitations of short-term trans-nasal tube feeding versus gastrostomy for long-term support.
Recognize the importance of family feasibility and caregiver training in feeding decisions.
Explore mixed feeding strategies to balance nutritional needs and oral experience.
key_facts: Oral feeding time exceeding 3 hours/day or intake supplying only 60-80% of needs with growth faltering are key escalation thresholds.
Unsafe swallowing with aspiration risk that cannot be mitigated is a critical trigger for enteral feeding.
Trans-nasal tube feeding (NG/NJ) is suitable for short-term stabilization, while gastrostomy is preferred for long-term supplementation.
Recurrent aspiration-related respiratory illness and caregiver exhaustion are important practical triggers for escalation.
Mixed strategies combining tube feeding with safe oral pleasure feeds can improve social participation and oral experience.
reference_quote: Thresholds used in neurologic impairment guidance include total oral feeding time exceeding about 3 hours per day, oral intake supplying only about 60-80% of individual needs with growth faltering, or any unsafe swallow where aspiration risk cannot be reduced to an acceptable level. (Source: ESPGHAN Advice Guide)
document_key_topics: Short-term versus long-term tube feeding options
Family feasibility and caregiver training in feeding decisions
Mixed feeding strategies for social and nutritional balance
